html:
Ice and Freeze-Thaw Damage Documentation for Springfield Commercial Roofs

The Ozarks don't get sustained deep winters the way the upper Midwest does, but Springfield has been through ice events severe enough to be remembered for years afterward — the region's major ice storm era in the mid-2000s is still the reference point a lot of longtime property managers here bring up when a forecast turns bad. Ice does different damage to a commercial roof than a windstorm or hail event, and it tends to show up in the drainage system before it shows up in the membrane itself.

Where Ice Damage Actually Starts

Roof drains and scuppers freeze first. Once a drain is blocked by ice, water backs up behind it with nowhere to go, and on a low-slope commercial roof that means ponding that sits under continued freeze-thaw cycling — expanding when it freezes, settling when it thaws, working at membrane seams and flashing details the whole time. We check every drain and scupper on a winter-damage inspection, across the whole roof rather than only the ones near visible ponding, because a partially blocked drain on the far side of the roof can be quietly causing damage nobody's noticed yet.

Parapet walls and roof-to-wall transitions are the other common failure point. Ice that forms against a parapet and then expands can separate flashing from the wall, opening a path for water that won't show as an interior leak until the next thaw sends it somewhere it shouldn't go.

How Freeze-Thaw Cycling Damages Membrane

A single hard freeze rarely splits a membrane on its own. It's the repeated cycling — water gets under a seam or into a small existing flaw, freezes and expands, thaws, and the cycle repeats over several cold snaps in a season — that turns a minor weakness into an open split. We look for this pattern specifically on older EPDM and modified bitumen roofs, where seam adhesive that's already aging is more vulnerable to this kind of cycling damage than a newer TPO or PVC system with heat-welded seams.

Structural Load From Snow and Ice Accumulation

Heavy wet snow followed by an ice layer on top adds real weight to a roof deck, and on older buildings or roofs with pre-existing structural concerns, that load is worth documenting even when the membrane itself looks undamaged. We note accumulation depth and any signs of deck deflection where it's visible, since a structural engineer may need that documentation if load concerns come up separately from the membrane claim.

Building the Documentation Package

Ice and freeze-thaw damage documentation looks different from a wind or hail report because the evidence is often in drainage patterns and membrane condition rather than obvious impact marks. We photograph drain and scupper condition, note ponding locations and depth, and document membrane splits or flashing separation with the same location-referenced approach we use for any storm claim, so the adjuster can follow the same trail whether the cause was ice, wind, or hail.

We're your roofing contractor, not a public adjuster — we document freeze-related roof damage so you and your adjuster can work from an accurate, complete scope rather than guessing at what a hard winter actually did to the roof.

If a winter storm also brought high wind, see our wind and storm damage claims page for that side of the inspection, or start at the commercial roof insurance claims page for the general process. Our insurance documentation support page covers how we build and hand off the record itself.

Snow & Ice Damage Claim FAQ

Can ice damage a roof even without a major ice storm?

Yes. Ordinary freeze-thaw cycling over a normal Ozarks winter can gradually damage membrane and drainage as effectively as one severe ice event, just more slowly and less visibly.

How do frozen drains cause roof damage?

A blocked drain lets water pond on the roof instead of draining off, and that standing water sits under continued freeze-thaw cycling that stresses membrane seams and flashing over time.

Is roof snow load something insurance covers?

Structural damage from snow and ice load can be a covered peril depending on the policy. We document accumulation and any visible deck issues so that determination can be made against the actual conditions.

How soon does ice damage need to be inspected after a storm?

As soon as it's safe to get on the roof. Ice and thaw cycles can continue causing damage through a season, so earlier documentation gives a clearer picture of what a specific event caused versus later cycling.

Do heat-welded membranes handle ice better than older systems?

Generally yes — heat-welded TPO and PVC seams tend to hold up better under freeze-thaw cycling than older adhered EPDM or modified bitumen seams, which is something we account for when assessing an older roof's vulnerability.
